questions.question: Which observability signal is best described as numeric measurements aggregated over time (counts, averages, percentiles) that is cheap, low cardinality, and ideal for dashboards and alerts?
options: Logs
Metrics
Traces
Spans
questions.explanation: Metrics are aggregated numeric measurements over time, cheap and low-cardinality, answering 'how is the system behaving overall.' Logs are discrete high-cardinality events, and traces are the causal request path across services.

questions.question: An outbound HTTP, SQL, or Service Bus call your service makes is recorded in App Insights as which telemetry type, landing in which KQL table?
options: Request, in the `requests` table
Dependency, in the `dependencies` table
Trace, in the `traces` table
Event, in the `customEvents` table
questions.explanation: Outbound calls are Dependency telemetry in the `dependencies` table. The `requests` table holds inbound server-side calls (request vs dependency is the inbound/outbound distinction).

questions.question: In Application Insights vocabulary, the `traces` table stores distributed traces (the correlated call tree of a request across services).
questions.explanation: The `traces` table holds log messages (typically `ILogger` output) with severity and properties — NOT distributed traces. A distributed trace is reconstructed from correlated `requests` and `dependencies` joined by `operation_Id`.

questions.question: Which App Insights field corresponds to the W3C/OTel TraceId — the single id for the whole end-to-end operation, propagated to every service?
options: `operation_ParentId`
`id`
`operation_Id`
`cloud_RoleInstance`
questions.explanation: `operation_Id` equals the W3C/OTel TraceId, the single id for the entire end-to-end operation. `operation_ParentId`/`id` map to parent/SpanId (this span and its caller), not the whole-operation id.

questions.question: What is the required and recommended way to configure App Insights ingestion in a modern .NET app?
options: Instrumentation-key-only configuration via `InstrumentationKey`
The connection string (`APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING`)
A storage account SAS token
An OTLP endpoint URL with no credentials
questions.explanation: The connection string is mandatory and carries the ingestion endpoint plus instrumentation key. Instrumentation-key-only configuration is deprecated/retired.

questions.question: Given the two log calls below, which statement is correct about how App Insights captures `orderId`?
questions.code:
logger.LogInformation($"Order {orderId} shipped");        // A
logger.LogInformation("Order {OrderId} shipped", orderId); // B
options: A captures `orderId` as a queryable `customDimensions.OrderId`; B flattens it into one string
B captures `orderId` as a queryable `customDimensions.OrderId`; A flattens it into one opaque string
Both capture `orderId` as a queryable property identically
Neither captures `orderId` as a queryable property
questions.explanation: B uses a named message-template placeholder, so `OrderId` becomes a queryable `customDimensions` property. A is string interpolation, which App Insights sees as one opaque flattened message string, losing the structure.

questions.question: A developer's `logger.LogInformation(...)` calls never appear in App Insights, while their warnings and errors do. What is the most likely cause?
options: Adaptive sampling is dropping all Information logs
The default App Insights logger capture level is `Warning`, so `Information` is dropped until you lower it
Information logs go only to Live Metrics and are never stored
The connection string is missing so only severe logs are sent
questions.explanation: The default capture level for the App Insights logger category is `Warning`; `Information` is silently dropped until you lower the level in configuration (e.g. `Logging:ApplicationInsights:LogLevel`). Sampling keeps whole operations together rather than dropping by severity, and a missing connection string would drop everything.

questions.question: On a hot path you need accurate per-minute counts of a custom measurement without inflating ingestion cost or risking throttling. Which API and why?
options: `TrackMetric` — it samples high-volume metrics automatically
`GetMetric().TrackValue()` — it pre-aggregates in-process (count/sum/min/max per minute), emitting far fewer items
`TrackEvent` — events are cheaper than metrics
`TrackMetric` — it pre-aggregates per minute before sending
questions.explanation: `GetMetric` pre-aggregates in-process per minute, so it emits far fewer telemetry items and is cheap. `TrackMetric` sends one item per call with no pre-aggregation — high ingestion volume and possible throttling; neither API is subject to sampling.

questions.question: In the W3C `traceparent` value `00-4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736-00f067aa0ba902b7-01`, what does the trailing `01` indicate?
options: The protocol version is 1
There is 1 child span
The trace-flags byte whose low bit marks the operation as sampled/recorded
The parent-id occupies 1 byte
questions.explanation: The final field is trace-flags; bit 0 (low bit) is the sampled flag, so `01` means recorded/sampled and `00` means not sampled. The version is the leading `00` field, not the trailing byte.

questions.question: With 5% fixed-rate sampling enabled, the query below reports roughly 1/20th of the real failures. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
requests
| where success == false and timestamp > ago(1h)
| summarize failures = count() by name
options: Replace `count()` with `sum(itemCount)` to re-weight by how many originals each item represents
Add `| extend failures = failures * 5` after the summarize
Replace `requests` with the `traces` table
Remove the `where` clause so no rows are filtered out
questions.explanation: Each retained item records `itemCount` (how many originals it represents); `sum(itemCount)` re-weights counts to stay approximately correct under sampling, while `count()` undercounts. Multiplying by a hardcoded factor or switching tables does not correctly account for per-item weights.

questions.question: Live Metrics Stream data is unsampled, streamed with ~1-second latency over a dedicated channel, and is not stored or retained for later KQL querying.
questions.explanation: Live Metrics uses a separate dedicated channel that is not sampled and not retained — data streams to the portal while you watch and is then discarded, so it does not count against ingestion/retention cost. KQL queries the stored workspace instead, with ingestion latency.

questions.question: Which of the following OpenTelemetry-to-Application-Insights mappings are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: OTel Span with kind=Server maps to Request telemetry (`requests`)
OTel Span with kind=Client maps to Dependency telemetry (`dependencies`)
OTel span attributes map to `customDimensions`
OTel resource attribute `service.name` maps to `operation_Id`
OTel TraceId maps to `cloud_RoleName`
questions.explanation: Server spans become requests, client spans become dependencies, and span attributes become `customDimensions`. `service.name` maps to `cloud_RoleName`/`cloud_RoleInstance` (not `operation_Id`), and TraceId maps to `operation_Id` (not `cloud_RoleName`).

questions.question: Across a fleet of microservices participating in one distributed trace, which sampling approach best preserves complete end-to-end transactions?
options: Adaptive sampling tuned independently per instance to a target items/sec
The same fixed-rate sampling with a traceid-based hash across all services
Ingestion (server-side) sampling configured differently per service
Disabling sampling on some services and enabling adaptive on others
questions.explanation: A consistent fixed rate plus a traceid-based hash keeps (or drops) an entire trace end-to-end, so transactions aren't half-kept. Per-instance adaptive tuning or mismatched/differing rates across services break end-to-end transactions because the sampled decision diverges.

questions.question: In the classic ASP.NET Core SDK, what must you do before configuring pure fixed-rate sampling?
options: Nothing — fixed-rate is the SDK default
Disable adaptive sampling first, because `AddApplicationInsightsTelemetry()` turns on adaptive sampling by default
Switch the connection string to an OTLP endpoint
Set the default log level to `Information`
questions.explanation: `AddApplicationInsightsTelemetry()` enables adaptive sampling by default, so to get pure fixed-rate behavior you must disable adaptive sampling first (e.g. `EnableAdaptiveSampling = false`) and then add the fixed-rate processor.

questions.question: A team wants to keep only the slow or failing traces and discard fast successful ones. Why can't the in-process App Insights SDK do this, and what can?
options: The SDK already does this; just enable adaptive sampling
SDK sampling is head-based (decides at the start of an operation), so it can't selectively keep slow/failing traces; tail sampling in the OTel Collector can
Fixed-rate sampling automatically keeps the slowest 5% of traces
Ingestion sampling keeps only failed operations by default
questions.explanation: App Insights SDK sampling is head-based: it decides at the start of an operation, before duration or outcome is known, so it cannot preferentially keep slow/failing traces. Tail sampling, which decides after the trace completes, requires the OpenTelemetry Collector — the in-process SDK cannot do it.

questions.question: To ensure a caught exception's full stack trace and type land in the `exceptions` table with proper correlation, how should you log it?
options: Concatenate `ex.ToString()` into the message string: `logger.LogError("Failed: " + ex)`
Pass the exception object as the first argument: `logger.LogError(ex, "Payment failed for order {OrderId}", orderId)`
Use `logger.LogInformation` so it isn't filtered by the default level
Call `TrackEvent("Exception")` with the message as a property
questions.explanation: Passing the exception as the first argument captures its type and stack trace and routes it to the `exceptions` table, while named placeholders remain queryable. Stuffing the exception into the message string loses the structured stack/type, and `TrackEvent` is for business events, not exceptions.

questions.question: Two services handle one request, but the portal shows them as separate, unjoined transactions over a normal `HttpClient` call. Which actions plausibly address the root cause? (Select all that apply.)
options: Ensure both services use W3C format (`Activity.DefaultIdFormat = W3C`) so `traceparent` propagates
Use the same sampling configuration on both services so the operation isn't kept on one side and dropped on the other
Set the App Insights default log level to `Information` on both services
Confirm `HttpClient` instrumentation is injecting the `traceparent` header on the outbound call
Replace the connection string with an instrumentation-key-only configuration
questions.explanation: Broken correlation is typically a propagation/format mismatch or a sampling mismatch: align on W3C, ensure `traceparent` is injected (auto via HttpClient instrumentation), and use the same sampling config so one side doesn't keep an operation the other drops. Log level affects what logs appear, not trace joining, and key-only config is retired and unrelated.

questions.question: You need a low-latency, cheap SLO threshold alert on server response time with ML-tuned baselines. Which alert type fits best, and why not the alternative?
options: A log (search) alert, because KQL queries run continuously with sub-second latency
A metric alert on pre-aggregated metrics, because it is low-latency and cheap and supports dynamic thresholds; log alerts run KQL on a schedule with more latency and per-evaluation cost
A log (search) alert, because only KQL alerts support dynamic thresholds
A metric alert, because metric alerts can express any arbitrary KQL condition
questions.explanation: Metric alerts fire on pre-aggregated metrics with low latency and low cost and support dynamic (ML-tuned) thresholds, ideal for SLO-style thresholds. Log alerts run a KQL query on a schedule — more flexible but higher latency and per-evaluation cost — so they're not the best fit here.
